---
title: "Randomisation"
output:
  beamer_presentation:
    theme: "default"
    colortheme: "default"
    fonttheme: "default"
  ioslides_presentation: default
  slidy_presentation: default
header-includes:
- \usepackage{hyperref}
- \usepackage{caption}
- \definecolor{links}{HTML}{2A1B81}
- \hypersetup{colorlinks,linkcolor=,urlcolor=links}
colorlinks: true
linkcolor: blue
urlcolor: blue
---

```{r, echo = FALSE}
library(knitr);
opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE);
```


# Introduction to the randomisation approach

\LARGE

Throughout this module, we have conducted hypothesis tests in a similar way:

- Calculate test statistic (e.g., t, F, Chi-square)
- Compare our calculated statistic to the theoretical null distribution \pause

P-value: Assuming the null hypothesis is true, what is the probability of getting the actual test statistic, or one more extreme than it, from this null distribution?


# Introduction to the randomisation approach

\LARGE

Null distribution makes several assumptions about the data:

- Normal distribution
- Equality of variances \pause

When these assumptions are violated, we then need to apply a transformation of some sort to the data, or to use a different non-parametric approach to testing our null hypothesis.


# Introduction to the randomisation approach

\Large


**If the ordering of the data we collected was actually random, then what is the probability of getting a test statistic as or more extreme than the one that we actually did?** \pause

- Build the null distribution by randomising our data in some useful way
- Conceptually, most students actually find randomisation methods easier to understand 
- More challenging to implement in practice


# An instructive example

\Large

![](img/fig_wasps.jpg)

Two fig wasps with (relatively) short ovipositors


# An instructive example

\Large

```{r, echo = FALSE}
library(knitr);
sp   <- c(rep("SO1", 17), rep("SO2", 15))
eg   <- c(3.256, 3.133, 3.071, 2.299, 2.995, 2.929, 3.291, 2.658, 3.406, 
          2.976, 2.817, 3.133, 3.000, 3.027, 3.178, 3.133, 3.210, 3.014, 
          2.790, 2.985, 2.911, 2.914, 2.724, 2.967, 2.745, 2.973, 2.560, 
          2.837, 2.883, 2.668, 3.063, 2.639);
wp   <- as.data.frame(cbind(sp, eg));
colnames(wp) <- c("Species", "Ovipositor length (mm)");
sp1 <- as.numeric(as.character(wp[wp[,1]=="SO1",2]));
sp2 <- as.numeric(as.character(wp[wp[,1]=="SO2",2]));
tmo <- t.test(sp1, sp2, var.equal = TRUE);
kable(wp[1:9,]);
```


# An instructive example

\Large

Standard approach would be to use a two sample t-test:

- **Null hypothesis:** the two means are the same
- **Alternative hypothesis:** the two means are not the same (two-sided) \pause

Check t-test assumptions:

- Data are normally distributed
- Both samples have similar variances

If data are not normally distributed, use a Mann Whitney test instead


# An instructive example

Calculate the t-statistic:


$$t = \frac{mean(SO1) - mean(SO2)}{s_{p}}.$$ \pause

The $s_{p}$ is just being used as a short-hand to indicate the pooled standard deviation,

$$s_{p} = \sqrt{ \left(\frac{(n_{1} - 1)s^{2}_{1} + (n_{2} - 1)s^{2}_{2}}{n_{1} + n_{2} - 2} \right)  \left(\frac{n_{1} + n_{2}}{n_{1}n_{2}} \right)}.$$

Values of n1 and n2 are the sample sizes for SO1 (17) and SO2 (15), respectively, and $s^{2}_{1}$ and $s^{2}_{2}$ are the sample variances for SO1 and SO2, respectively.


# An instructive example

\Large

Mean ovipositor lengths:

- SO1: 3.0301176 mm
- SO2: 2.8448667 mm \pause

Calculate our t-statistic, then get p-value with jamovi:

- t-statistic (30 df): 2.4190497
- p-value: 0.0218352 \pause


Hence, we would reject our null hypothesis and conclude that the difference between the group means is statistically significant.


# An instructive example

\Large

**Randomisation approach**: If there really is no difference between group means, then we should be able to randomly shuffle group identities (species) and get a difference between means that is not far off the one we actually get from the data. 

1. Shuffle the identities all of the species  \pause
2. Calculate the mean between shuffled groups  \pause
3. Repeat steps 1 and 2 many times  \pause
4. Compare the shuffled mean differences to the actual one \pause

[This app](https://bradduthie.shinyapps.io/randomisation/) illustrates how the process works.


# An instructive example

![](img/random_hist.png)

# An instructive example

\large

Comparing the observed distance to the random mean differences:

- Observed difference was 0.185251
- 213 random differences were more extreme (above 0.185251 or below -0.185251) \pause
- Probability of value as or more extreme

$$P = \frac{213 + 1}{9999 + 1}$$ \pause

Compare the randomisation and traditional approaches:

- Randomisation P value: 0.0214
- Traditional t-test p-value: 0.0218


# Randomisation is a general tool

\Large

```{r, echo = FALSE}
library(knitr);
sp   <- c(rep("SO1", 17), rep("SO2", 15))
eg   <- c(3.256, 3.133, 3.071, 2.299, 2.995, 2.929, 3.291, 2.658, 3.406, 
          2.976, 2.817, 3.133, 3.000, 3.027, 3.178, 3.133, 3.210, 3.014, 
          2.790, 2.985, 2.911, 2.914, 2.724, 2.967, 2.745, 2.973, 2.560, 
          2.837, 2.883, 2.668, 3.063, 2.639);
wp   <- as.data.frame(cbind(sp, eg));
colnames(wp) <- c("Species", "Ovipositor length (mm)");
sp1 <- as.numeric(as.character(wp[wp[,1]=="SO1",2]));
sp2 <- as.numeric(as.character(wp[wp[,1]=="SO2",2]));
tmo <- t.test(sp1, sp2, var.equal = TRUE);
kable(wp[1:9,]);
```


# Randomisation is a general tool

\Large


```{r, echo = FALSE}
library(knitr);
sp   <- c(rep("SO1", 17), rep("SO2", 15))
eg   <- c(3.256, 3.133, 3.071, 2.299, 2.995, 2.929, 3.291, 2.658, 3.406, 
          2.976, 2.817, 3.133, 3.000, 3.027, 3.178, 3.133, 3.210);
hl   <- c(3.823, 2.948, 2.641, 2.346, 2.406, 2.928, 3.128, 2.187, 4.017, 
          3.495, 3.338, 3.176, 2.863, 2.405, 3.646, 3.064, 3.510);
wp   <- as.data.frame(cbind(hl, eg));
colnames(wp) <- c("Head length (mm)", "Ovipositor length (mm)");
kable(wp[1:9,]);
```



# Randomisation is a general tool

```{r}
plot(x = hl, y = eg, xlab = "Head length (mm)", ylab = "Ovipositor length (mm)",
     pch = 20, cex = 1.5, cex.lab = 1.25, cex.axis = 1.25);
corv <- round(cor(hl, eg), digits = 3);
text(x = 3.8, y = 2.6, labels = paste("r = ", corv, sep = ""), cex = 2);
```


# Randomisation is a general tool

```{r}
plot(x = hl, y = eg, xlab = "Head length (mm)", ylab = "Ovipositor length (mm)",
     pch = 20, cex = 1.5, cex.lab = 1.25, cex.axis = 1.25, col = "grey80");
corv <- round(cor(hl, eg), digits = 3);
text(x = 3.8, y = 2.6, labels = paste("r = ", corv, sep = ""), cex = 2, 
     col = "grey80");
rop <- sample(x = eg, size = length(eg), replace = FALSE);
points(x = hl, y = rop, pch = 20, cex = 1.5, col = "red");
corv <- round(cor(hl, rop), digits = 3);
text(x = 3.8, y = 2.5, labels = paste("r = ", corv, sep = ""), cex = 2, 
     col = "red");
points(x = hl, y = rop, cex = 1.5);
```


# Randomisation is a general tool

```{r, echo = FALSE}
iter    <- 9999;          
rval    <- NULL;          
for(i in 1:iter){   
    ora     <- sample(x = eg, size = length(eg), replace = FALSE);
    rval[i] <- cor(hl, ora);
}
hist(rval, xlab = "Randomised correlation coefficient", col = "grey", 
     main = "", cex.lab = 1.5, cex.axis = 1.5, breaks = 20);
truecor <- cor(hl, eg);
corv <- round(cor(hl, eg), digits = 3);
arrows(x0 = truecor, x1 = truecor, y0 = 500, y1 = 10, lwd = 3, length = 0.1,
       col = "red");
text(x = truecor, y = 550, cex = 1.5, labels = "Observed", col = "red");
extrcor <- sum(abs(rval) >= abs(truecor));
Pval    <- (1 + extrcor) / (9999 + 1);
pvalue  <- cor.test(eg, hl)$p.value;
```



# Randomisation is a general tool

\Large

After randomising ovipositor length values 9999 times, `r extrcor` values more extreme than observed correlation (i.e., > `r corv` or < `r -corv`) \pause

$$P = \frac{`r extrcor` + 1}{9999 + 1} = `r Pval`.$$ \pause

Traditional Pearson product moment p-value:

$$P = `r pvalue`.$$



# Assumptions of randomisation tests

\LARGE

Assumptions of two sample t-test:
    
1. Data from independent groups
2. The data are normally distributed
3. Observations independent of each other
4. Both samples have similar variances \pause

With randomisation, we do not need to assume 2-4. 

# Assumptions of randomisation tests

\LARGE

The downsides of randomisation

- Statistical inferences are limited to the sample (not the population) 
- This is because no formal assumption of random sample from populations \pause
- Can still make the verbal argument generalising from sample to populations [1, 2]


\footnotetext[1]{Ernst, M D. 2004. \textit{Stat. Sci.} \href{https://doi.org/10.1214/088342304000000396}{19:676-685}.}
\footnotetext[2]{Ludbrook, J, \& H Dudley. 1998. \textit{Am. Stat.}, \href{https://www.jstor.org/stable/2685470}{52:127-132}.}


# Bootstrapping confidence intervals

\Large

```{r, echo = FALSE}
library(knitr);
sp   <- c(rep("SO1", 17), rep("SO2", 15))
eg   <- c(3.256, 3.133, 3.071, 2.299, 2.995, 2.929, 3.291, 2.658, 3.406, 
          2.976, 2.817, 3.133, 3.000, 3.027, 3.178, 3.133, 3.210, 3.014, 
          2.790, 2.985, 2.911, 2.914, 2.724, 2.967, 2.745, 2.973, 2.560, 
          2.837, 2.883, 2.668, 3.063, 2.639);
wp   <- as.data.frame(cbind(sp, eg));
colnames(wp) <- c("Species", "Ovipositor length (mm)");
sp1 <- as.numeric(as.character(wp[wp[,1]=="SO1",2]));
sp2 <- as.numeric(as.character(wp[wp[,1]=="SO2",2]));
tmo <- t.test(sp1, sp2, var.equal = TRUE);
```

**We can use randomisation to calculate confidence intervals (CIs)**

Recall the traditional approach to calculating CIs:


- Upper CI: mean + constant * standard error
- Lower CI: mean - constant * standard error \pause

Calculating standard error:

$$SE = \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}.$$

Above, s is the standard deviation and n is the sample size.


# Bootstrapping confidence intervals

\Large

**We can use randomisation to calculate confidence intervals (CIs)**

Recall the traditional approach to calculating CIs:


- Upper CI: mean + constant * standard error
- Lower CI: mean - constant * standard error

Finding the right constant

- Appropriate z score or t score 
- Encompasses correct probability density

E.g., 95 per cent of standard normal distribution between z = -1.96 and 1.96


# Bootstrapping confidence intervals

\Large

Values of SO1 ovipositor length: 

`r sp1` \pause

- Mean: `r round(mean(sp1), digits = 4)`
- Standard error: `r round(sd(sp1)/sqrt(length(sp1)), digits = 4)`
- t-score for df = 17 - 1: 2.120

```{r, echo = FALSE}
UCI <- round(mean(sp1) + 2.12*sd(sp1)/sqrt(length(sp1)), digits = 3);
LCI <- round(mean(sp1) - 2.12*sd(sp1)/sqrt(length(sp1)), digits = 3);
```

\pause

**Upper CI:** `r round(mean(sp1), digits = 4)` + 2.120(`r round(sd(sp1)/sqrt(length(sp1)), digits = 4)`) = `r UCI`

**Lower CI:** `r round(mean(sp1), digits = 4)` - 2.120(`r round(sd(sp1)/sqrt(length(sp1)), digits = 4)`) = `r LCI`


# Bootstrapping confidence intervals

\Large

**Randomisation approach**: 

- Resample the data *with replacement* many times \pause
- Approximate what would happen if we went out and collected more data from the population \pause
- Get the distribution of means from hypothetical resampling [1] \pause
- Rank random means, get lower 2.5\% and upper 97.5\% of ranked values

\footnotetext[1]{Manly, B F J. 2007. \textit{Randomization, Bootstrap and Monte Carlo Methods in Biology} (3rd ed.). Chapman \& Hall/CRC.}


# Bootstrapping confidence intervals

Original SO1 ovipositor length values:

3.256, 3.133, 3.071, 2.299, 2.995, 2.929, 3.291, 2.658, 3.406, 2.976, 2.817, 3.133, 3, 3.027, 3.178, 3.133, 3.21 \pause

SO1 ovipositor values resampled *with replacement*

3, **3.027**, 2.929, **3.291**, **3.291**, 2.976, **2.299**, **2.299**, **3.027**, **3.027**, **3.133**, **3.027**, **2.299**, **3.133**, **2.299**, 3.178, **3.133**

- Bold values appear multiple times in resampling
- Original values 3.256, 3.071, 2.995, 2.658, 3.406, 2.817, and 3.21 do not appear at all in resampling \pause

Now we calculate the mean of our values resampled with replacement 10000 times.


# Bootstrapping confidence intervals


![](img/boot_hist.png)


# Monte Carlo on spatial data

We can also use randomisation when a null distribution cannot be derived from the data.


![](img/fig_tree.jpg)


**Are fig trees are randomly distributed across a sampling area?**


# Monte Carlo on spatial data


\Large

```{r, echo = FALSE}
fpet <- read.csv("data/FpetTrees.csv");
lat1 <-  28.289;
lat2 <-  28.2918;
lon1 <- -113.1145;
lon2 <- -113.1095;
kable(head(fpet));
```


# Monte Carlo on spatial data

![](img/tree_locs.png)


# Monte Carlo on spatial data

\Large

**Are the trees randomly distributed in the brown box?**

- Cannot randomise latitude and longitude coordinates
- Want to compare actual tree distribution with randomly placed trees
- Monte Carlo compares observed test statistic with one derived from theoretical null [1]
- Use mean distance to nearest neighbour as a test statistic

\footnotetext[1]{Manly, B F J. 2007. \textit{Randomization, Bootstrap and Monte Carlo Methods in Biology} (3rd ed.). Chapman \& Hall/CRC.}


# Monte Carlo on spatial data

\Large

**Null Hypothesis:** Mean nearest neighbour distance for a focal tree in the observed data will not differ significantly from the mean nearest neighour distance obtained from the same number of trees randomly distributed within the sampling area.

1. Randomly place 59 trees in the sampling area
2. Calculate mean nearest neighbour distance of random trees \pause
3. Repeat steps 1 and 2 many times
4. Generate a null distribution of nearest neighbor distances \pause
5. Compare actual mean nearest neighbour distance will null distribution


# Monte Carlo on spatial data

![](img/random_trees.png){width=80%}

# Monte Carlo on spatial data

![](img/monte_hist.png)
